frame_label: Motherhood as Basis of Respect / Women Defined by Male Relations
generated_comment: Amira beta, I know you're smart, independent, and we're proud of your work. But you are a daughter of this house, *kisi ki behen ho*. When people look at you, they see our family, our values. We just want you to have the *izzat* you deserve, and for our family's *izzat* to remain intact. Don't forget that.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment, while seemingly caring and pro-woman ('we're proud of your work', 'we want you to have the izzat you deserve'), frames Amira's worth and the 'respect' she receives primarily through her relational identity as 'a daughter of this house' and 'kisi ki behen ho' (someone's sister). Her individual actions are presented as reflecting directly on the family's honor (*izzat*), rather than on her own individual merits, aligning with the 'Women Defined by Male Relations' core belief and 'relational framing' linguistic cues.
original_frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
persona_text: You are Khalid. Age 55. Married. A Muslim man in Delhi. Your niece Amira who is 24 and lives with your family has started wearing western clothes Ñ jeans, fitted tops, no hijab. She works at an NGO and says her workplace is diverse and informal. You have been tolerating this for six months but last week she came to a family function dressed this way in front of your elderly mother and several conservative relatives. You are calling Amira into the room now. You are telling her what happened at the function, what your mother said afterward, what the relatives are saying, and what the conditions are under which she can continue to live in your house.
